title: Module 9: Decisional Balance
learning_objective: Explore pros and cons of both changing and staying the same; Give equal attention to both sides of ambivalence; Avoid using decisional balance to persuade; Let the patient draw their own conclusions
scenario_context: Learn to explore ambivalence by examining both sides of change decisions about weight loss

persona_config.name: Well,
persona_config.background: Recognizes there is a problem and is considering making changes but has not yet committed.

dec_1.persona_text: I know I need to lose weight, but I just don't know if I'm ready to make big changes to my eating habits.

choice_points.option_text: Clearly the benefits outweigh the downsides. Your health depends on it.

feedback.immediate: This tries to argue the patient out of ambivalence rather than exploring it.
feedback.learning_note: Ambivalence is normal in change - our role is to explore it, not resolve it.

choice_points.option_text: You're feeling torn about this. What are some of the good things about how you eat now?

feedback.immediate: Excellent - acknowledges ambivalence and starts by exploring one side openly.
feedback.learning_note: Ambivalence is normal in change - our role is to explore it, not resolve it.

choice_points.option_text: You're just making excuses. You know what you need to do.

feedback.immediate: This treats normal ambivalence as character flaw, damaging the relationship.
feedback.learning_note: Ambivalence is normal in change - our role is to explore it, not resolve it.

dec_2a.persona_text: I do know it's important, but you don't understand - food is one of the few pleasures I have after a stressful day.

choice_points.option_text: You need to find other ways to deal with stress. Food shouldn't be a reward.

feedback.immediate: This continues the argumentative dynamic and increases resistance.
feedback.learning_note: Watch for patient resistance signals - defensiveness or disengagement.

choice_points.option_text: Food is important comfort and pleasure for you, especially after difficult days. That makes sense. What else do you value about your current eating pattern?

feedback.immediate: Good recovery - validates the patient's perspective and continues exploring non-judgmentally.
feedback.learning_note: Validation acknowledges the patient's experience without judgment.

dec_2b.persona_text: Well, I enjoy eating. I love trying new foods and restaurants. It's something I do with friends and family. Food is social for me.

choice_points.option_text: Those things aren't important compared to your health. You need to prioritize.

feedback.immediate: This devalues what matters to the patient, creating discord.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Food connects you to people you care about and brings joy to your life. Those are real and important benefits. What concerns you, if any, about your current eating habits?

feedback.immediate: Perfect - gives equal weight to both sides without judgment.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: That's nice, but you can still have those social experiences with healthier food choices.

feedback.immediate: The 'but' dismisses what the patient just shared as important.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

dec_3a.persona_text: I guess I also just don't have to think about it. I eat what I want, when I want. There's freedom in that.

choice_points.option_text: There's something valuable about that spontaneity and freedom. And what, if anything, concerns you about your weight or eating?

feedback.immediate: Good - continues to validate before exploring the other side.
feedback.learning_note: Validation acknowledges the patient's experience without judgment.

choice_points.option_text: But that 'freedom' is causing health problems. Let's focus on what you need to change.

feedback.immediate: This doesn't give adequate exploration to the status quo side.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

dec_3b.persona_text: Well, I do worry about my health. My doctor said I'm pre-diabetic. And I can't fit into my favorite clothes anymore, which is depressing.

choice_points.option_text: Exactly! So clearly you need to change your eating habits. The health risks are serious.

feedback.immediate: This tries to use the patient's concerns to argue rather than continuing balanced exploration.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Your health concerns and how you feel about your body are weighing on you. What might be some benefits if you did make changes to your eating?

feedback.immediate: Good - acknowledges concerns and explores potential change benefits.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

dec_4a.persona_text: I guess I'd feel better physically. Maybe have more energy. And I wouldn't have to worry as much about diabetes getting worse.

choice_points.option_text: So there are real benefits to changing. Now let's make a plan for how you'll change your diet.

feedback.immediate: This moves too quickly to action without fully exploring the ambivalence.
feedback.learning_note: Ambivalence is normal in change - our role is to explore it, not resolve it.

choice_points.option_text: Better energy and health, less worry about diabetes - those would be meaningful. What concerns you about making changes to your eating?

feedback.immediate: Excellent - continues balanced exploration by looking at costs of change.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

dec_5a.persona_text: I'm worried I'll feel deprived. That I won't be able to enjoy meals with friends. And honestly, I'm not sure I have the willpower to stick with it.

choice_points.option_text: Those downsides aren't as important as your health. You're overthinking this.

feedback.immediate: This treats the patient's legitimate concerns as unimportant.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Feeling deprived, losing that social connection, doubts about following through - those are real concerns. So on one side there's enjoying food freely and social connection, and on the other there's health and feeling better. Where does all this leave you?

feedback.immediate: Perfect - gives equal weight to both sides and lets patient draw conclusions.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Let's focus on the positives. Don't think about what you're giving up.

feedback.immediate: This tries to wish away ambivalence rather than working with it.
feedback.learning_note: Ambivalence is normal in change - our role is to explore it, not resolve it.

dec_6a.persona_text: I guess... I'm realizing that I care about both. Maybe I don't have to give up the social part? Maybe there's a way to change some things without losing what I value?

choice_points.option_text: You're finding that it doesn't have to be all or nothing - you might be able to hold onto what matters while making some changes. What might that look like?

feedback.immediate: Excellent - reflects the emerging resolution and invites exploration of possibilities.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Right, you can still go out with friends, just order salads instead.

feedback.immediate: This takes over the problem-solving instead of letting the patient generate ideas.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

dec_end_good.persona_text: Maybe I could focus on portion sizes when I'm out with friends? Or choose one or two meals a week to be more careful about? That way I don't lose the social part but I'm still making some changes.

dec_end_poor.persona_text: I don't think you understand what I'm dealing with. I'll think about it.
